data.title: Ellington Financial: Little Impact On Book Value From April Volatility Expected
data.description:
Introduction

So far in 2025, Ellington Financial (NYSE:EFC) has outperformed the VanEck Mortgage REIT Income ETF (MORT), delivering a total return of ~12%:

Even after the recent share price gains, EFC trades at a small book value discount. What is more, the company's common stock dividend is covered by earnings, which I expect to increase in the next few years thanks to a steeper yield curve (principally driven by incremental Fed rate cuts).

In this article, I outline EFC's portfolio, recent operating performance, and the outlook for Fed policy, ultimately ranking EFC a Buy. I also discuss risks stemming from a U.S. recession, fewer-than-expected Fed rate cuts, and the likelihood of significant near-term losses during financial market turmoil.

Company Overview

You can access all EFC results on its website here. Ellington Financial is a mREIT specialized in credit investments (86% of equity, respectively 81% of assets are allocated to this strategy). The firm also operates the Longbridge platform, which originates reverse mortgages. It accounts for 12% of equity, respectively 13% of assets. Last but not least, EFC has a small agency securities portfolio where only 2% of equity and 6% of assets are allocated:

The credit portfolio is quite dynamic and varied, with management exhibiting discretion over its composition in time. As of the end of Q1 2025, the main allocation was to various types of residential mortgage loans (circa 70% of the portfolio), followed by commercial mortgages (16%):

The company's enterprise value stands at ~$16.1 billion, of which 8% is funded with common equity, 2% with preferred shares, leaving the remaining 90% for net debt.

Q1 2025 Results Overview

In Q1 2025, EFC reported adjusted distributable earnings attributable to common shareholders (a metric which strives to present earnings available for distribution, i.e., it most notably adds back realized and unrealized gains/losses) of $0.39/share, down 22% Q/Q as the prior quarter result was boosted from abnormally large unrealized losses.

On a GAAP basis, EPS stood at $0.35/share, up 40% Q/Q, benefiting from higher net interest income (which increased thanks to a lower cost of short-term funding following recent Fed rate cuts) as well as higher other income.

The company's book value stood at $13.44/share at the end of Q1 2025, down 0.6% Q/Q as EFC issued common stock during the quarter while also paying a common share dividend of $0.39/share.

Post-quarter end, the company disclosed that it estimated its book at $13.47/share at the end of April 2025, indicating the recent market volatility has had little effect on underlying performance.

Outlook for Fed policy

Futures markets price in 0.5% in Fed rate cuts by December 2025, with another 0.5% in Fed easing likely to be delivered by December 2026, ultimately taking the Fed funds rate to 3.25-3.50%:

As such, the effect of lower funding costs, which boosted performance in Q1 2025, is likely to be further amplified in the next few years. Of course, I do expect long-term interest rates to decline as well, but the net effect should be positive for EFC as I only anticipate interest rates on long-term securities to decrease by circa 0.5% from current levels, lower than the 1% decrease in the Fed funds rate by December 2026.

All in all, as the Fed makes progress with its policy normalization, we should see a steeper yield curve, which will boost performance at mREITs such as EFC, which largely borrow short-term and invest in longer-duration securities.

Company Valuation

Ellington Financial trades at a ~4% discount to its estimated April 2025 book value of $13.47/share, while the current dividend of $0.13/month remains covered by underlying earnings. As such, while the valuation is clearly not distressed, I believe the shares present a compelling investment opportunity in light of the expected boost to earnings from future Fed rate cuts.

The company is also increasing its allocation to commercial mortgages (+1% share in the portfolio relative to year-end 2024), seeking to benefit from significant opportunities in the space (as explained in my article on Claros Mortgage Trust (CMTG) here). I like management's active portfolio rotation, which will likely allow it to outperform more conservative mREITs.

To conclude, while the share price has largely recovered from the April 2025 correction, I do believe EFC will deliver attractive returns to shareholders over the next few years, ranking it a Buy.

Risks

Given EFC's overall focus on residential mortgage securities, the principal risk facing the company is a U.S. recession, which will likely be accompanied by higher unemployment and an increase in homeowner delinquencies. Indeed, the May 2025 U.S. jobs report showed a 0.2% M/M drop in the labor force participation rate, which, coupled with negative revisions of recent employment numbers, points to weak U.S. economic momentum.

Fed rate cuts may also be derailed by continuing uncertainty on trade policy or recent oil price increases. As things stand, however, the market's overall expectations align with the Fed's summary of economic projections, which put the Fed funds rate at about 3.4% at the end of 2026.

Due to the significant amount of leverage employed, mREITs such as EFC are highly exposed to financial markets volatility and a breakdown in typical correlations during times of extreme stress. For instance, the company's share price slumped by ~79% during the March 2020 COVID-19 crash.

Conclusion

Ellington Financial delivered solid Q1 2025 earnings, with the mREIT benefiting from higher net interest income and higher other income on its diversified investment portfolio.

Looking ahead, the Fed is widely expected to deliver incremental rate cuts later in 2025 and into 2026. I think that this will result in a steeper yield curve, boosting earnings at mREITs such as EFC, which borrow short-term and invest in long-duration securities.

Against this backdrop, EFC shares still trade at a small book value discount, despite the company covering its generous dividend with adjusted distributable earnings attributable to common shareholders. As such, even after the outperformance relative to the VanEck Mortgage REIT Income ETF so far in 2025, I believe a Buy rating is warranted for EFC.

Investors should consider risks stemming from a potential U.S. recession, fewer than anticipated Fed rate cuts, and significant near-term losses during times of financial markets volatility.

Thank you for reading.

data.title: Wall Street Week Ahead
data.description:
Listen on the go! A daily podcast of Wall Street Breakfast will be available by 8:00 a.m. on Seeking Alpha, iTunes, Spotify.

Wall Street will have a busy week ahead, with market participants keeping a close eye on the escalating conflict between Israel and Iran and any further trade developments. Meanwhile, traders will also see a Federal Reserve monetary policy decision and a busy economic calendar.

Over the weekend, Israel said it was striking military targets in Tehran and advised Iranians to evacuate weapons factories. Iran also launched more missiles against Israel. Oil prices (CL1:COM)(CO1:COM) will be on watch, and so will gold (XAUUSD:CUR).

The Fed will deliver its fourth interest rate decision of the year on Wednesday, and the Monetary Policy Committee is widely expected to leave the key policy rate unchanged. The spotlight will be on its updated economic projections, the first since the "Liberation Day" tariffs, and Chairman Jerome Powell's press conference.

In terms of economic data, the May U.S. retail sales report is due on Tuesday. Housing starts and building permits are on the docket on Wednesday.

Earnings spotlight: Monday, June 16: Lennar (LEN). See the full earnings calendar.

Earnings spotlight: Tuesday, June 17: Jabil Inc. (JBL), Kirkland's (KIRK), Vince (VNCE). See the full earnings calendar.

Earnings spotlight: Friday, June 20: Accenture (ACN), Kroger (KR), CarMax (KMX). See the full earnings calendar.

In case you missed it

Stock picks for a multipolar world

Jamie Dimon sees chance economic numbers 'deteriorate soon'

Tariff casualty: free shipping

Lawmakers went big on stock trades as Trump unveiled 'Liberation Day' tariffs

Quantum stocks get a Jensen bump

Gundlach on unsustainable U.S. debt

Don't mess with this ETF

Cathie Wood on Trump v. Musk

data.title: Week Ahead: Geopolitics, Tariffs, And Central Banks
data.description:
The response of the US dollar and Treasuries to Israel's attack on Iran and the palatable risk of escalation was uninspiring. It supports the popular narrative about the changing role of the US dollar and assets in the world economy. US stocks and bond sold off ahead of the weekend. It should not be regarded as a "smoking gun" but part of an incremental addition to the accumulating evidence. The G7 meeting in Canada (June 15-17) may command attention, but if it can agree on a statement, it will be likely so bland to express the divergence of views (and the apparent isolation of the US) that it likely have limited impact in the capital markets. The US dollar has tended to weaken when the Trump administration presses its tariff offensive. Last week, President Trump reiterated that in a week or two, letters to US trading partners will be sent that carry the new bilateral tariff. Also, Trump threatened to boost the tariff on autos, which would seem to only complicate efforts to reach trade deal with Japan and Europe. A couple of months ago, the administration was touting 90 deals in 90 days. So far, there is one and that is with the UK, which indicated that none of it was enforceable. Vietnam and maybe India deals could be next.

The week ahead features several central bank meetings, including the Federal Reserve, Bank of Japan, Norway's Norges Bank, and the Bank of England. All four are likely to leave rates on hold. The Fed will update its economic projections. The Fed funds futures are not quite pricing in the two hikes that the median projection anticipated last December and March would be appropriate this year. The markets will also be looking for some guidance on the unwinding of the Fed's balance sheet (QT). The Swedish and Swiss central banks also meet. They are expected to cut key rates. A quarter-point cut would bring the Swedish policy rate to 2% and could prove to be the floor. The Swiss National Bank is more interesting. The EU harmonized measure of consumer prices has fallen by 0.2% over the past 12-months. The deposit rate is at 0.25%. There is some speculation that it could return to a negative policy rate. We think this is unlikely. It is still an important threshold, and the exchange rate suggests little sense of urgency is required.

US

Drivers: The dollar's initial reaction to Israel's strike on Iran seemed relatively muted and this may make some question the dollar's safe haven status, and it feeds into the narrative that the dollar's role in the world economy may be changing. The FOMC meeting is important, not because it will do anything, which it will not. But what it says can still move the financial markets. The Summary of Economic Projections, even taken with a high of uncertainty, is the baseline for market. The Fed funds and swaps markets move around the median Fed projection. Yet it seems that the market converges with the Fed rather than the other way around. Trade appears to be distorting Q2 GDP but favorable unlike the large drag on Q1, but we think this is concealing a weakening of consumer demand amid the slowing of the labor market, resumption of student loan servicing, elevated debt-stress levels, and a high-degree of uncertainty. The Treasury's monthly portfolio report may be of greater interest than usual. It covers April, when the narrative hard turned to capital flight from the US, and some drew parallels between the dollar selling off despite rising rates and emerging market currencies. Within the context of the America's large net international investment position, falling asset markets and falling dollar suggests some foreign selling but the markets would have performed similarly if it were domestic, dollar-based investors diversifying offshore, too.

Data: Real sector due this week looks soft. The slump in auto sales (15.65 mln seasonally adjusted annual rate vs 17.27 in April) were a drag on headline retail sales though the measure that excludes auto, gasoline, building materials and food services may have risen after a 0.2% decline in April. Industrial output may have eked out a small gain in May after a flat report in April. May housing starts look little changed after a 1.6% increase in April. NY and Philadelphia June surveys will be reported. There will also be much interest in the April Treasury's International Capital report given the narratives of foreign capital flight after the reciprocal tariffs were announced. Recall that in Q1 25 foreign investors bought far more US assets than in Q1 24 (~448 bln vs $37.6).

Prices: After falling to new three-year lows on June 12 near 97.60, the Dollar Index rebounded to about 98.60 in response to Israel's strike on Iran. It needs to overcome resistance near 98.70 to spur a return to the 99.40-100.00 area. The 20-day moving average is 99.20 and DXY has not closed above it since May 19, and the down trendline from mid-May intersects slightly above there on Monday.

EMU

Drivers: Late last year, the 30-day rolling correlation of changes in the exchange rate and the two-year US-German rate differential was above 0.80, a multiyear high. It fell to less than 0.10 in early June and rarely has been lower in the last few years. It is now near 0.25. Not only is the ECB well ahead of the Fed in the easing cycle, but large pools of capital seemed under-weight, the opposite of the US.

Data: The market expects a pause in the ECB's easing cycle before one more cut before the end of the year. In this context, the April current account, construction spending, and Germany's ZEW survey pose headline rise at best.

Prices: Israel's attack on Iran halted the euro's rally after it reached slightly above $1.1630 on June 12 and snapped the four-day advance. Still, despite the setback ahead of the weekend, the June 12 low (~$1.1485) held. A break of last week's lows in the $1.1370 area is needed to signal anything important technically. The 20-day moving average is a little higher (~$1.1385) and the euro has not settled below it in almost a month.

PRC

Drivers: There are only a handful of currencies that have been more stable against the dollar this year than the Chinese yuan. It has appreciated by about 1.75% against the dollar this year. This is not by accident. It may not always be clear how or why, but Beijing manages to deliver a fairly stable exchange rate. The growth of the US, Europe, and Japan in the 1950s and 1960s was facilitated by stable exchange rates. This proved too rigid, and Nixon's closing of the gold window was only the details of the coup de grace that was already well under way. Expect the yuan to continue to track the greenback, which means, in a falling dollar environment, it will trade softer on the crosses, making the offshore yuan a potentially attractive funding currency for carry trades.

Data: China report real sector data for May early Monday. The PMIs warn of poor data. The tariff war with the US is only adjusted toward the middle of the month, though reports suggest some container shipments were orders by US retailers before the Geneva agreement. Still, by the end of May, containers activity at the West Coast ports had ground to a near-halt again. Retail sales and industrial production may have slowed sequentially, house prices do not appear to have reached a bottom, but officials seem reluctant to take fresh fiscal measures or monetary measures. The loan prime rates will remain steady at 3.0% and 3.50% for the one-year and five-year tenors.

Prices: The dollar slipped below CNH7.17 on June 12 to record the low for the week before rebounding ahead of the weekend to almost CNH7.19. The high last week was near CNH7.20. The upper end of the consolidative range is CNH7.2240-60, and a foothold above CNH7.20 would target it next. The PBOC set the dollar's reference rate at nearly a two-month low before the weekend (CNY7.1772), its lowest level in two months. It lowered the dollar's fix for the past four sessions, which limits the dollar's upside. This suggests officials are not resisting slow yuan appreciation, though we imagine their tolerance is not without limits.

Japan

Drivers: The Bank of Japan is wrestling with trying to normalize monetary policy while the economy remains weak (-0.7% quarter-over-quarter contraction in Q1), and inflation remains elevated. As part of its effort to adjust monetary policy, it has slowed the purchases of government bonds, which appears to be adding to the pressure at the long end of the curve. At the same time, the changes in the exchange rate and in the US 10-year yield are around 0.27 over the past 60 sessions after being more than twice as high for most of Q1 25. On the other hand, the rolling 60-day correlation of the changes in the yen and Dollar Index is slightly below 0.90, the highest in 30 years.

Data: There are two highlights in the coming days. The first is the BOJ meeting. Governor Ueda can be expected to reiterate that if the economy evolves as the central bank expects, it will raise rates further. The swaps market does not have more than 10 bp of tightening priced in until October, and even by the end of the year, the market no longer fully discounts a quarter-point hike. Recall that as recently as the end of Q1, the swaps market reflected expectations for at least 75 bp of hikes. A couple of days after the BOJ meeting, the BOJ will meet with primary dealers to ostensibly discuss supply and demand for long-term JGBS. May's CPI will also be released. Officials, like market participants, have a good idea of it from the Tokyo CPI that was reported on May 30. The headline was stable at 3.4%, but the core measure rose to 3.6% (from 3.4%) and the measure that excludes fresh food and energy rose to 3.3% (from 3.1%). The takeaway if BOJ officials, like market participants, understand that price pressures are still rising. The April national core measure, which the target of policy, was at 3.5%, the highest since January 2023.

Prices: The yen initially rallied to new highs for the week in response to news of Israel's attack on Iran but then succumbed to the broadly firmer dollar and modest rise in US interest rates. The dollar recovered from around JPY142.80 to reach almost JPY144.50, a few ticks shy of the previous day’s high. The month's high was set in the middle of last week near JPY145.45.

UK

Drivers: Sterling was lifted to new three years highs last week, not by constructive developments in the UK, where the data mostly surprised on the downside but broadly weaker US dollar. The rolling 30-day correlation between changes in sterling and changes in the Dollar Index reached almost 0.90 and the 60-day correlation has been hovering around 0.80 for more than a month. The forward guidance from Bank of England officials had arguably persuaded market participants that the central bank will slow walk this part of the easing cycle, but the data may force its hand, even if not in the coming week. Since the end of April extreme, the swaps market has seen the expected year-end base rise slightly more nearly 35 bp before the disappointing data. It has been halved to about 17 bp above the late April low and it is falling. The 10-year Gilt yield has risen by a net of about five basis points.

Data: It is a busy week for the UK but back loaded. May CPI is due Wednesday. Utility prices jumped in April, and this was reflected in a 1.2% month-over-month surge in measures consumer prices and will not be repeated. Still, anything more than a 0.3% increase in May will lead to a higher year-over-year pace (3.5% April). The following day the Bank of England meets. There is virtually no chance of a change in policy. The base rate will remain at 4.25%. The swaps market is discounting about a 63% chance of a cut at the next meeting in August but does not have the next cut fully discounted until November. At the end of the week, May retail sales are due. They will likely slow from the heady 1.2%-1.3% increase in April.

Prices: Sterling set a new three-year high slightly before Israel's attack a little above $1.3630. It subsequently was sold to almost $1.3515, and while it took out the previous day's low (~$1.3525), it settled back within the range, neutralizing the negative technical implications. Support may extend toward last week's lows (~$1.3455-65).

Canada

Drivers: The US dollar's broad movement appears to have eclipsed other influences on the movement of the Canadian dollar. The rolling 30- and 60-day correlations of changes in the Canadian dollar and the Dollar Index are near 0.75 and 0.68, respectively, near the highest of the year. It is notable, too, that more than oil, short-term interest rates, and risk more broadly (S&P 500 as proxy) changes in the Canadian dollar are more correlated with change in gold (30-day correlation is around 0.62 and the 60-day correlation is near 0.57).

Data: They highly include April portfolio flows and retail sales. Through March, foreign investors were net sellers of about C$10 bln of Canada's stocks and bonds. In Q1 24, they were net buyers of C$24 bln Canadian assets. Still, in Q1 25, the Canadian dollar was virtually flat but in Q1 24, it fell by about 2.25%. March retail sales (0.8% month-over-month) were flattered by auto sales, without which retail sales fell by 0.7%. Early indications from StatsCan suggest that April retail sale may have risen by 0.5%. Still, this week's reports are unlikely to change market views that there less than a 1/3 chance of a Bank of Canada rate cut next month. The swaps market is discounting one cut fully in H2 25.

Prices: The Canadian dollar was one of the two G10 currencies that appreciated against the greenback ahead of the weekend. The other being the Norwegian krone. While many cite the rally in crude oil, we note that 1) the 60-day correlation of changes in the exchange rate and oil is low (less than 0.10) and is actually slightly inverse over the past 30 days, and 2) the Canadian dollar typically does better on the crosses in a firm US dollar environment. The greenback recorded a new eight-month low ahead in the North American session ahead of the weekend near CAD1.3565. It recovered to almost CAD1.3600 in late turnover. There appears little chart support ahead of the CAD1.3475-CAD1.3500 area, though the momentum indicators will likely be over-extended before it reaches it.

Australia

Drivers: Over the past 30 sessions, the changes in the Australian dollar are more correlated with changes in the Dollar Index than the Canadian dollar (~0.72 vs 0.65), but the 60-day correlation is the opposite. Changes in the Australian dollar are more correlated with changes in the Canadian dollar (~0.70) than the Dollar Index (~0.55). Changes in the Aussie and gold have a 0.60 correlation over both periods.

Data: There is one report coming in days. It is the May jobs report on June 19. The Australian labor market looks fairly stable through April. Overall, almost 104k jobs were created in the first four months of the year, down from 127k in the January-April 2024 period. Of these jobs, a little more than 2/3 (~68k) were full-time positions, down from closer to 3/4 in the first four months of 2024. The 4.1% unemployment rate is unchanged from last April, although the participation rate has risen from 66.7% to 67.1%. Ahead of the data, the futures market has about an 80% chance of another cut at next central bank meeting on July 8.

Prices: The Australian dollar was sold to a seven-day low near $0.6455 amid the initial risk-off response to Israel's strike on Iran. However, it recovered to around $0.6515 in North America before stalling. It had recorded a seven-month high slightly shy of the $0.6550 retracement level we targeted in the middle of last week. The Aussie finished the week nearly flat. On the year, the Australian dollar is the worst performing G10 currency with a little less than a 4.9% gain.

Mexico

Drivers: The Mexican peso is benefitting from a weakening US dollar, attractive carry, liquidity, and relatively low volatility. The total returns this year (carry+spot) is almost 15%. Leaving aside the Russian ruble, only three other emerging market currencies have generated a better total return (Brazilian real ~18.2%, Hungarian forint ~17.5%, and Czech koruna 15.2%). The peso's three-month implied volatility near 12.2% is near the middle of these currencies, and its liquidity is far superior.

Data: Mexico's economic calendar is extremely light in the coming days. Still, ahead of the central bank meeting on June 26, the central bank will see April retail sales and the IGAE economic activity report (similar to a monthly GDP report), May's trade balance, and the first half of June CPI. Despite the May CPI being above the upper end of the target range (2%-4%), the markets look for another (the fourth) half-point cut. Separately, Brazil and Chile's central banks meet this week. Chile's policy meeting is on June 17, a day before the FOMC meeting concludes and Brazil's central bank meeting. Chile's central bank's easing cycle began in July 2023, with its policy rate at 11.25%. It has been at 5.0% since the last rate cut in December 2024. May's inflation stood at 4.4%. Although the easing cycle may not be over, the central bank will likely remain on hold this week. On the other hand, Brazil's central bank began its tightening cycle last September and raised the Selic rate by 175 bp in 2024 and another 250 bp through last month. Softening price pressures and a weakening economy has given rise to speculation that the hikes are almost over. The swaps market is discounting one more quarter point hike.

Prices: Over the past couple of years, the peso has seen sharp dramatic selloffs on geopolitical, and more recently, tariff developments but they rarely sustained for more than a day. This may speak to the peso's use as a proxy for other less liquid emerging market currencies. The pattern was repeated before the weekend. The greenback settled near MXN18.88 on June 11 after recording a 10-month low in the middle of the week (~MXN18.8265) and spiked to around MXN19.1030 on Israel's attack. It fell back to around MXN18.8850 in North America ahead of the weekend. A break of MXN18.80 could spur a move toward MXN18.60.

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: M&T Bank Is Now A Modest Buy At Current Valuation And Earnings Potential
data.description:
A Regional Bank Whose Price Has Climbed, But Remains A Modest Buy Opportunity

It's time to talk about regional banks again, so today I'm following up on a bank I last covered in July 2023, M&T Bank Corp (NYSE:MTB), whose SA profile describes it as a Buffalo NY-based regional bank that has multiple segments including "commercial Bank, retail bank, and institutional services and wealth management."

After I called it a "strong buy" in my last coverage, it seems the market saw it that way too and since then, the stock is up +33%, as of today's article writing.

What I loved about this stock so much the first time was an attractive dividend yield, and proven net income growth, for example. However, this time I applied an updated approach looking at 6 weighted categories.

Based on my updated research and data, I found that this time I am pulling back to just a modest buy recommendation rather than a strong buy this time.

My thesis argues that this bank's positive upside factors include growth in its loan and trust business, strong margins within its sector, proven dividend growth and positive EPS growth estimates, and strong credit ratings with a low D/E. At the same time, the macro environment right now creates an air of uncertainty for regional banks, not to mention the systemic risk of exposure to unrealized losses on bonds.

Sector Still Challenged By Loan Demand, Rates, And CRE Exposure

Let's talk about the bigger picture of regional banks.

What I think are key factors that can impact this sector are factors like loan demand in the economy, interest rates /Fed decisions, demand for wealth advisory services, but also exposure to commercial real estate and the question of bad loans on a bank's books.

From a more recent article I found by S&P Global this May, "the analyst community has adopted a more cautious stance on US bank loan growth this year amid a slew of macroeconomic wildcards."

The article went on to include M&T Bank on a list of banks whose loan growth estimates have declined, with M&T seemingly having the worst forecast:

I believe once we return to a period of ultra-low interest rates again, it could boost loan demand as debt will be cheaper, but since Fed decisions can impact interest rates overall, let's consider that CME FedWatch data shows a high probability the Fed will not be lowering its target rate at its upcoming June 18th meeting, and also at least a 76% chance it will stay that way after the July meeting too.

In terms of exposure to commercial real estate/CRE, the FDIC said in its 2025 risk review of the entire sector that "higher interest rates weighed on the CRE sector and increased refinancing risk for CRE borrowers."

I believe that is something investors should consider as a potential downside risk for this sector.

Because M&T also has a wealth advisory segment, I think a positive future upside factor could be if they grow that segment further.

Consider what global consultancy McKinsey said in their Feb. 2025 research study:

We estimate that by 2034, at current advisor productivity levels, the advisor workforce will decline to the point where the industry faces a shortage of roughly 100,000 advisors.

This market demand in the wealth space could drive that segment for M&T, however I would also remind readers that this is a space crowded by some big names, including Morgan Stanley (MS) and Raymond James (RJF), who are also looking to manage those client monies and earn fees.

M&T also has competition for investors who may be comparing the best-performing regional banks, so for peer comparisons today I picked Huntington Bancshares (HBAN), Regions Financial (RF), and KeyCorp (KEY).

The larger picture for this sector, based on the evidence gathered above, paints a slightly less optimistic picture for me so I gave it a low hold rating in this category.

Revenue Growth Signs In Both Loan And Non-Loan Segments, Is A Positive Upside Factor

We got a taste of this sector and macro environment, so now let's talk about any company-specific data pointing to top-line revenue growth potential.

From Q1 results, l would highlight the data below showing that loans grew slightly by 1% YoY, driven primarily by growth in commercial (non CRE) and consumer loans, while it seems CRE has scaled back:

While a 1% growth may seem small, it also comes on the heels of data from the 2024 annual report which said that "on average, commercial and industrial loans grew by $4.6B or eight percent in 2024, and average consumer loan growth contributed an additional $2.1B in balances."

So, despite some of those macro forecasts of weaker loan demand estimates, M&T has continued to grow loans and I believe this is an upside as it can drive future interest income.

In addition, notable to mention from Q1 results is that non-loan segments like trust and brokerage also saw YoY growth, which shows that M&T is at least somewhat diversified beyond depending on loans:

At the same time, though, I would say M&T itself is not among the large financial brands in the advisory and brokerage space, so according to its official site it partners with another firm, LPL Financial (LPLA) to fulfill certain products since LPL is both a registered investment advisor and broker-dealer.

From a future view, I believe having such a strategic partnership can drive M&T to bring more of its banking clients into the advisory/brokerage segment, further growing that side of the business.

For the multiple reasons above, I am modestly bullish in this category.

Attractive Profit Margin Within Sector, As YoY Expense Growth Is Minimal

In terms of the key metric I think will impact the bottom line, it is the net income margin/profit margin, which is an impressive margin of nearly 31%, easily beating the sector average of 22%.

If I were investing in this stock and put greater weight on the bottom line, this would certainly be an upside factor, as the firm has proven its efficiency at turning a profit.

Further, compared to the peers I selected, MTB is on the high end of this group in terms of profit margin:

Looking beyond just the margin itself, to make sense what could be impacting this bank's cost structure, I turned to Q1 results and the table below which shows the bank's YoY growth of total non-interest costs only rising 1%, particularly due to a nice drop in other costs of operations, and other factors:

This seems like a very minimal YoY growth in non-interest expenses, and that along with the attractive margins compared to peers is a reason I am highly bullish in this category.

Attractive Balance Sheet Profile With Strong Credit Ratings and Low D/E

When it comes to balance sheet risk to think about, first I would point out that the overall company has solid investment-grade credit ratings from several major rating agencies, according to its website:

This should appeal to investors looking for a bank with a lower-risk profile that has coverage from the agencies.

The last rating note I found from Fitch, for example, from last fall, said a key driver of their rating was that "MTB's risk profile is more conservative than its peers, which is a credit strength. Its focus on stability over growth is creditor-friendly."

Second, Q1 results data shows that the ratio of non-accrual (non-performing) loans vs total loans has declined on a YoY basis, and remained below 1.5%:

This is another positive upside factor, showing an improving trend when it comes to bad loans on the books.

Third, when it comes to debt vs equity at this bank, M&T is on the low end of this peer group I selected, with a D/E of just 0.41.

My opinion is that the combination of the points I raised above, such as the strong credit ratings, the low D/E ratio among peers, and improvement to non-performing loans on the books, justifies a strong bullish call in this category of balance sheet risk.

A Bank That Is a Proven Dividend Grower, And Future Earnings Could Drive It Further

Another impressive category to mention is proven dividend growth.

Although this bank's 3, 5, and 10 year dividend growth CAGR is somewhat below the sector average, it has been positive. For instance, its 10 year CAGR is 6.79%, and to visualize this I would point to the 10 year trend in the chart below:

As positive net income is a key component of this firm's cashflow statement, and cashflow supports the dividends, I believe that continued earnings growth ought to justify continued hiking of the dividend. From the analyst consensus, it seems the EPS is expected to grow between 6 - 16% over the next 3 years, and that plus the strong profit margins I pointed out earlier could make the case that there is a decent chance this firm could keep growing its dividend under such conditions.

I am bullish in this category, but only modestly since this bank did not beat its sector average on dividend growth.

A Chance To Buy At Modest Valuation, As Future Price Targets Look Promising

In all my recent articles, I made the case that there can be a relationship between valuation, earnings estimates, and future price targets, so that is what this section is about.

To consider a forward-looking valuation metric, I picked the fwd P/E (GAAP based) multiple which is 11.54, relatively in line with the sector average this time. That tells me the market mostly expects this bank to perform in line with its sector and is not overvaluing it.

That coincides with the price chart I created below, where this stock is most recently trading below its 200-day simple moving average, and far below its 5-year high, so not a ton of investor bullishness to show here lately:

This could be an opportunity to buy at a modest valuation, because when applying the fwd P/E multiple to consensus EPS estimates, my future share price targets all expect upside, with a 3-year avg price gain expected to be +17%.

Because of this potential upside to the price, along with the modest valuation it is trading at now, I am bullish in this category.

However, let's also consider the consensus price targets from major financial media like the Wall St Journal, for example, as a comparison, and their avg price target of $202 if it came true would be a potential +13% gain on today's close price of $178.

Challenge To My Outlook: Systemic Risk Of Unrealized Losses

A challenge to my bullish outlook, not mentioned yet or included in the rating table, could be that there is a systemic risk of other banks with exposure to a large pool of bonds in their asset portfolio, that have lost value.

Consider what Fortune magazine said in mid-May about this risk:

U.S. banks held $482.4 billion in total unrealized losses on securities investments at the end of 2024, according to Federal Deposit Insurance Corporation data, an increase of $118 billion, or 32.5%, from the previous quarter.

These unrealized losses don’t show up on a bank’s income statement unless the assets are sold, but they represent a looming threat to liquidity if depositors get spooked.

So, even if M&T is or is not directly impacted, its share price can see downside if other regional banks start running into the above issue of liquidity, driving investors to flee all regional banks and thereby making my bullish case overly optimistic.

Conclusion Gives Slight Downgrade From Prior Strong Buy Rating

To conclude today's article, my updated research has made the case to pull back from my prior strong buy rating and give this stock just a buy this time.

In the rating table below, I gave extra weight to the two categories I though deserved it the most and that was the profit margin and the balance sheet risk.

With an avg. rating score of 7.97, it puts this stock just in the "buy" range:

My Methodology

I use 6 steps to picking stocks for future growth, focusing on dividend-income stocks trading on major US exchanges, across multiple sectors, and providing commentary across multiple fundamentals with specific attention to data from the income statement, balance sheet, and cash flow statements.

data.title: Kesko Oyj: A Hidden Nordic Retail Giant At A Very Reasonable Valuation
data.description:
Kesko Oyj (KKOYF)(KKOYY) is one of the leading retailers in Northern Europe that has been flying under the radar with international investors. The company transformed itself from a small grocery retailer into what is now a sprawling retail empire dominating multiple verticals. Its main focus continues to be groceries, but it now also operates DIY building and home improvement products stores, along with consumer and fleet car sales. It is a dominant player in Finland, but has expanded to nearby markets such as Sweden, Norway, Denmark, and beyond.

In recent years, the company has strategically invested in digital platforms, logistics infrastructure, and geographic expansion to ensure it maintains a strong competitive moat and that it is well-placed to capture market growth. While it is not a high-growth company, it offers an attractive dividend and has potential for profit margin improvement. It can be an interesting company to follow for investors searching to diversify with international companies operating in stable regions.

Company Overview

As previously mentioned, Kesko is a familiar brand in Finland and some Nordic countries, but it flies under the radar when it comes to international investors. The company can be thought of as a mix of Walmart (WMT), Home Depot (HD), and to a lesser extent CarMax (KMX). Kesko operates three grocery store brands, mostly in Finland, and has expanded to more than 1,100 stores across the country.

Kesko has been quick to adopt online shopping, with online grocery now accounting for a meaningful percentage of revenue. Importantly, Kesko has followed the strategies of the likes of Costco (COST) and Kroger (KR), giving high importance to developing its own private labels, which now account for roughly 20% of total grocery sales. In Finland, Kesko has the second-largest market share with approximately 33%, and over 40% of the online grocery market. Kesko also operates a B2B food service business that caters to restaurants, hotels, and other similar businesses, similar to what Sysco Inc. (SYY) does. The grocery segment is delivering a relatively high operating margin, compared to that of other similar peers.

The second key business segment for Kesko is Building & Technical Trade [BTT], which caters to both professional trades people as well as DIY home improvement customers. It can be thought of as a similar business to Home Depot or Lowe's (LOW). This business operates in eight countries, and currently has a relatively low operating margin, but management has shared their expectations that they can reach their 6-8 % EBIT margin target in a few years. This business is being expanded mostly through bolt-on acquisitions in Northern Europe, and its sales are roughly two-thirds to building professionals and one-third to DIY customers.

Finally, the car trade business segment sells used and new cars. This segment is performing well, but its net sales represent only around 10% of total group net sales.

Bolt-on Acquisitions

Kesko has followed a strategy of M&A growth, in particular for its building and trade [BTT] business segment. Acquisitions have tended to be relatively small and easy to integrate, a kind of roll-over expansion strategy. This has allowed Kesko to quickly gain market share in new markets. A good example is the recent purchase of family-owned Danish retailer Davidsen A/S for €427 million in 2024, one of Denmark's largest building and home improvement retailers. This acquisition was further reinforced with additional smaller acquisitions in 2025 that will be integrated into the Davidsen subsidiary.

First Quarter of FY2025 Results

Kesko's net sales increased modestly to €2.8 billion in the first quarter of FY2025, representing steady year-over-year progress despite this being traditionally Kesko’s slower quarter. Interestingly, the BTT and Car Trade divisions emerged as strong performance standouts. Both showed robust top-line growth, with BTT delivering impressive B2B dynamics, and the Car Trade defying unmistakable market downturn trends. This shows Kesko’s ability to diversify earnings effectively, improving resilience.

Working capital and inventories increased significantly, though largely due to seasonal reasons. The balance sheet remains reassuringly solid, underlined by debt metrics comfortably below management’s targeted ceiling. Margins in the grocery segment have been pressured as the company invests in pricing competitiveness, which is positioning Kesko as consumers’ top choice for affordable quality. This is a case of taking some short-term pain for long-term gain, and there are early signs that the strategy is working with improvements seen in footfall and online basket size.

The Building & Technical Trade is already benefiting from the Danish acquisitions, offering refreshed growth momentum and significant potential for profit margin expansion. As for the Car Trade segment, it continues gaining market share as it benefits from some popular electric vehicle launches from trusted automobile brands.

Financials

Kesko is one of the companies facing a post-pandemic pull-back, as consumers initially splurged after the pandemic lockdowns. This pulled forward demand, which resulted in a subsequent growth decline. Still, it appears things are now stabilizing for the company, and its operating margin remains quite healthy for a retailer.

To put things in perspective, Kesko's operating margin of ~4.6% is higher compared to global peers such as Walmart, Ahold Delhaize (OTCQX:ADRNY), or Tesco PLC (OTCPK:TSCDY). Also, according to management, they expect margins to improve in the medium to long-term.

Kesko might not be a wonderful business, but it is a pretty decent one, with returns on equity of ~15%, and returns on capital employed [ROCE] and returns on invested capital [ROIC] of close to 10%. This might not be the most attractive franchise in the world, but it is a solid business earning very decent returns on its capital employed.

Balance Sheet

Kesko is also managed in a very prudent way, with relatively low leverage and a solid balance sheet. For comparison, global peers like Walmart and Tesco are currently operating with higher debt-to-EBITDA and debt-to-asset multiples.

Global Peers

Kesko Oyj also stands out among global peers, with some of the highest profit margins, exceeding those of companies like Walmart, Costco, and Kroger. Still, as is the case with most retailers, profit margins are low in absolute terms, and below those of U.S. home improvement leader Home Depot.

Shareholder Returns

One key attraction for income investors is that Kesko has a generous dividend policy of distributing between 60% to 100% of its comparable earnings per share. This approach gives the company more flexibility to adjust dividends as earnings change, so investors have to accept that it is normal for the dividend to be lower if earnings experienced a decline compared to the previous year.

At current prices shares are offering a dividend yield that is close to the ten-year average of ~4.7%, and there is potential for the dividend to be increased again if profit margins and earnings start trending higher once again.

Future Outlook

During the first quarter earnings call, management shared their expectation for operating profit to slightly improve in 2025, although the operating environment is expected to remain relatively challenging. Kesko estimates that its comparable operating profit in 2025 will amount to €640 million to €740 million. The profit guidance is based on an estimate of a gradually improving economic cycle in all Kesko operating countries. The company is still operating below its long-term financial targets, which call for a 6% operating margin, and return on capital employed [ROCE] above 14.5%.

The grocery and car trade business segments already exceed this ROCE target, it is the building and technical trade [BTT] segment which is lowering the group's average. Still, there is hope that as this business gets increased operating leverage from the acquisitions, it will be able to improve its financial performance.

Valuation

Trading at a roughly 23.9x GAAP P/E multiple, Kesko shares are not exactly cheap. Still, the company's strong brand, resilient cash generation, and solid track record justify, to a certain degree, a premium valuation. Still, Kesko will have to deliver the promised margin improvement and grow volume to continue justifying this P/E multiple.

Kesko appears expensive when compared to European peers like Tesco and Ahold Delhaize, but compared to U.S. peers like Walmart it looks like a bargain, trading at less than half its EV/EBITDA and Price to Cash Flow from Operations multiples.

Risks

While grocery stores are relatively stable businesses, often considered consumer staples, the company does have several important risks for investors to consider. First, competition in the grocery sector is intense, with operators in Finland like S Group and Lidl competing aggressively on price, which have pushed Kesko to lower prices too. In the Car Trade segment competition is also fierce, both with regional automotive dealerships and international car manufacturers. For its part, the BTT segment is highly cyclical and sensitive to economic conditions. Higher interest rates have dented construction and remodeling activity, impacting this business segment.

Other important risks include potential integration issues, especially international acquisitions, which might have different business cultures. Finally, tariffs and low consumer sentiment pose risks, particularly to the more discretionary or cyclical businesses such as the BTT and Car Trade segments. The risks are mitigated by Kesko's strong brand, particularly in the Nordic countries, a solid balance sheet, and healthy cash generation.

Conclusion

Kesko Oyj is an interesting company that investors interested in international companies should keep on their radar. While the company is currently not cheap, its valuation is very reasonable, and its core grocery business is highly resilient to economic cycles. The company can be particularly interesting to income investors, especially those wishing to diversify their dividends to other currencies, as Kesko has a policy of distributing most of its earnings as dividends. We believe the current valuation is reasonable, but for the time being we are happy to just keep an eye on the company in case an opportunity comes to buy shares at a significant discount to fair value.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: AMD: Catching Up In AI Doesn't Make It Undervalued
data.description:
Advanced Micro Devices (AMD) has outperformed the market by double-digits, since we recommended selling it, given its overvalued nature. The company has recently announced a $6 billion share buyback and continues to generate substantial profits. Despite that, AMD is an overvalued company that has a minimal ability to generate shareholder returns.

AMD Q1 Financial Results

The company has more than $7 billion in revenue in the quarter, substantial growth as its data center has outperformed.

However, the company still trades at roughly 7x annual revenue, an incredibly lofty valuation with its $190 billion market cap. While peer Nvidia is at 17x annual revenue, we also feel that the valuation of peers is incredibly lofty, as they both face risks. That's a valuation that needs substantial cash flow that can be directed towards shareholder returns. It's also worth noting that the rapid revenue growth seen with the company and datacenters appears to be slowing down.

The company has managed to do this revenue growth while growing its margins. The company's GAAP margins are 50% and the company's non-GAAP margins came in at 54%. The company has been supported by the Ryzen processor, which has become a strong CPU offering that's very competitive with what's offered by Intel.

That has enabled the company to capture increased market share.

The company's slowing growth is visible through 36% YoY growth but a 3% QoQ revenue decline. Even QoQ the company also saw a 1% decline in its GAAP margins. At the same time, the company's expenses, even with the gross margin, remain quite high. That means that the company's net income, despite strong QoQ performance, came in at $700 million.

That puts the company at a P/E of almost 70x. That's tough for a company that declined QoQ.

AMD Segment Performance

The company's segment performance also shows some concern with the company's valuation given that the company would need to hit 4x profits to justify its valuation.

The company's client & gaming did well off of new CPU and GPU releases but the TAM here for the company is dramatically smaller. Embedded has seen consistent weakness, and shrunk in size, though operating income remains strong. Still these are two segments that we don't see a path to justifying the company's valuation with their growth.

That leaves the rapidly growing data center market as the source of potential growth for the company. Here is where the concerns lie. The company saw substantial YoY growth after 2024 was a great year. However, QoQ the company saw a 5% revenue decline and 19% operating income decline. That implies that the company has had to trim margins here to preserve revenue.

Given the slowing down of data center growth, and the company's largest customers building their own CPUs, we don't see this segment ever providing a path to the company justifying its valuation.

AMD Artificial Intelligence

Now, it's worth noting that AMD is investing in a number of AI startups, and introducing the MI350X.

AMD has acquired startups such as Enosemi and Untether designed to help it improve its AI silicon and software offerings around AI. While these offerings will benefit the company, it's worth noting that AMD's 2025 acquisition count is lower than financially wealthier peer Nvidia. As a result, we don't expect this to set it ahead.

Now the MI350 will be a competitive processor and in a rack format will fit 128 processors with 33 kW of power. On a 3nm process versus 4 nm for Blackwell along with high memory (288 gB HBM3E) that makes it a strong processor for AI related tasks. MI350 benchmarks aren't out yet, but the MI325X was on par with Hopper and 1/3 of Blackwell.

Given the strong talked about compute of the MI350 vs MI325X we expect it to be competitive. However, in our view, parity between AMD and Nvidia especially with AMD's investments in these AI startups, is more of a sign of the lofty valuations.

Specifically, these companies need lofty margins to justify their valuation and aggressive revenue growth. However, their primary customers are the mega tech companies (Amazon, Google, etc.) who are all building their own GPUs. Competition between Nvidia and AMD will encourage both to lower margins from current lofty levels, which will hurt profits.

Combined with increased competitors and slowing down margin growth, we see profits never hitting the level required to justify investment.

AMD Shareholder Returns

The company has managed to build up its cash position versus prior years, but it still remains low versus its valuation.

The company had $727 million in FCF, but spent more than that on shareholder returns including share repurchases. This higher than the company's FCF rate is an only 1.5% shareholder return rate. That came with several billion in total debt increases, and it indicates how the company's ability to drive future returns will be tough without substantial growth.

Thesis Risk

The largest risk to our thesis is that AMD is working to capture market share from its rivals. It's market share in GPU is 8% versus more than 90% for Nvidia. Capturing substantial market share from peers Nvidia etc. with lower cost offerings could enable the company to drive more substantial shareholder returns and cash flow in excess of the company's current valuation.

Conclusion

AMD has outperformed the market since the last time we recommended selling it. Despite that, we recommend investors not being fooled by the company's valuation. The company's modest share buybacks are only 1.5% annually and the company barely afford it with its current FCF. The company's total debt load has continued to grow as well.

The company had a strong 2024 but QoQ it's clear that the company's growth rate is slowing down. Despite a strong showing with consumer CPUs and GPUs, the datacenter is the only market large enough to justify the company's valuation and growth there is slowing down rapidly. That slowdown in growth will hurt the company's ability to drive additional returns.

Let us know your thoughts in the comments below.

data.title: Alpha Picks Weekly Market Recap
data.description:
Hello and welcome to our weekly recap. I’m Steven Cress, Head of Quantitative Strategies at Seeking Alpha.

This week, global events took center stage. We saw renewed escalation in the Middle East, a tragic plane crash in India, and an encouraging agreement between the U.S. and China all impacting markets. In this newsletter, we’ll provide insights into these developments and also highlight notable movements within the Alpha Picks portfolio.

I’m also excited to announce the recent launch of our latest Quant product: the PRO Quant Portfolio (PQP). PQP is an actively managed, data-driven model portfolio that harnesses Seeking Alpha’s proprietary Quant system to deliver high-conviction stock ideas with disciplined execution. I encourage all readers to explore this new offering and discover the unique advantages it brings.

Thank you for joining us and I hope you enjoy this week's edition.

Israel-Iran Conflict Roils Global Markets

Regional tensions escalated when Israel launched military action against Iran, causing significant turbulence in global financial markets. Major averages opened lower, with the S&P 500, Nasdaq, and Dow all falling in pre-market. The sell-off also included European and Asian equities, as well as regional markets in the Middle East.

Oil prices saw substantial increases. Brent crude futures (CO1:COM) surged up to 13% in early trading, before moderating later in the trading session. Traditional safe-haven investments gained traction as investors sought stability; both gold prices and the US dollar strengthened.

Source Link

Investors remain concerned the conflict could constrict oil supplies, particularly through the Strait of Hormuz, which is a critical thoroughfare for global energy transport.

Boeing Shares Sink After Fatal Air India Dreamliner Crash

In Ahmedabad, India, the catastrophic crash of an Air India Boeing 787-8 Dreamliner killed nearly all people on board, marking the first fatal Dreamliner crash reported. Boeing's (BA) shares trended lower in the aftermath, along with Indian carriers like IndiGo and SpiceJet. The incident intensifies scrutiny on Boeing's safety record after a litany of challenges over the past several years.

Geopolitical Events

Following two dates of negotiations in London, the U.S. and China reached a preliminary trade agreement on Monday. The deal, which is still awaiting final approval, would see China relax export restrictions on rare earth minerals and magnets. In turn, the U.S. would ease restrictions on advanced technology exports to China and loosen visa restrictions for Chinese STEM students. The markets responded with cautious optimism.

Amid periods of shifting trade policy and geopolitical uncertainty, Quant offers tools that simplify stock picking by analyzing financial data to highlight stocks with the strongest investment fundamentals. Alpha Picks offers a more efficient approach to smarter investing, leveraging Seeking Alpha's powerful quantitative stock-picking system to deliver two data-driven stocks picks each month, helping investors save time and focus on high-quality opportunities.

GICS Sector Performance

Energy (XLE) led sector performance this week, as oil prices jumped following Israel's attack on Iran, with renewed concerns about potential oil supply disruptions.

Major deals in the Healthcare sector ignited sector-wide gains.

Industrials (XLI) finished lower for the week as concerns over lingering trade tensions weighed on the sector.

Crypto & Currencies

Bitcoin (BTC-USD) fell as much as 3% during early Friday trading, following renewed tensions in the Middle East.

The US dollar (DXY) continued its downtrend for the week, sinking to its lowest level in three years amid market turbulence over shifting US trade policy. However, the currency experienced a slight rally Friday morning following a rush to safe-haven assets.

Alpha Picks

What is Alpha Picks? Alpha Picks provides two monthly data-driven stock picks delivered straight to your inbox. Alpha Picks has collectively gained 168%, compared to the S&P 500’s 60% since its inception in July 2022.

The portfolio continues to significantly outperform, delivering a trailing three-month return of 18.39% compared to 8.03% for the S&P 500. AP stocks in the Financial and Industrial sectors led performance for the week.

United Natural Foods Inc. (UNFI) reported Monday that it had suffered a major cybersecurity breach, forcing the company to take key systems offline and disrupting order fulfillment at major retailers. This was announced one day before UNFI’s strong Q3 FY2025 earnings were announced, with net sales rising 7.5% to $8.06 billion and adjusted EBITDA increasing 20.8%. Despite these stellar results, the breach took a toll on share prices, which declined by double digits for the week. This decline, despite the company’s fundamental strength, creates a potential buying opportunity for investors looking for a bargain.

For investors with a fixed amount of capital who prefer a higher frequency of trading, consider our recently launched PRO Quant Portfolio (PQP). PQP is an actively managed, data-driven model portfolio that leverages Seeking Alpha’s proprietary Quant system to deliver high-conviction stock ideas through a disciplined, systematic rebalancing process. With weekly alerts, transparent buy/sell recommendations, and a focus on outperforming the Invesco S&P 500 Equal Weight ETF (RSP), PQP empowers active investors seeking long-term capital appreciation decisions without the need for constant capital infusions or manual intervention.

Economic Rundown

  • FOMC Outlook: Markets are pricing in a 97% chance the Federal Reserve will keep interest rates unchanged at 4.25%-4.50% during next week’s FOMC meeting. Rate cut expectations have now shifted to September, where there is a 56% probability rates will be cut 25bps.

  • Inflation: The May CPI rose 0.1% M/M, coming in below consensus forecasts. Year-over-year, the index rose 2.4% in May, up from April’s 2.3%, but still below the 2.5% expected.

  • Labor Market: Initial jobless claims held steady at 248K for the week ended June 7, while continuing claims rose to 1.956M, both above consensus expectations. The four-week moving average reached its highest level since August 2023, suggesting some softening in the U.S. labor market.

Seeking Alpha Earnings Calendar

data.title: FTAI Aviation: A Roller Coaster Ride In 2025
data.description:
Shares of aircraft and aircraft engine management concern FTAI Aviation Ltd. (NASDAQ:FTAI) have been on a wild ride in 2025. A short report from research concern Muddy Waters to start 2025 is largely responsible, claiming its shift from an emphasis on aviation leasing to its higher-margin MRO work is simply accounting shenanigans. With some of its aircraft being sold to a joint-venture – in which it participates – with exclusivity on all MRO work accruing to FTAI, the recent insider buying merited a deeper dive. An analysis follows below.

Company Overview

FTAI Aviation Ltd. is a Cayman Islands domiciled, New York City headquartered owner, lessor, seller, manufacturer, and repairer of aviation equipment and aftermarket engine components. At YE24, its portfolio held 109 aircraft and 312 engines. With roots dating back to 1946, FTAI was founded in 2014 as Fortress Transportation and Infrastructure Investors and went public in 2015, raising net proceeds of $351.1 million at $17 per share. A series of transactions were effectuated in 2022 to change its structure from an LLC to a corporation, at which point the current moniker was adopted. Shares of FTAI trade just under $124.00 a share and sport an approximate market capitalization of $12.7 billion.

Approach

The company’s business consists of two operating segments: Aviation Leasing and Aerospace Products. Aviation Leasing is fairly straightforward: acquire aircraft and aircraft engines and subsequently market them for lease or sell, further supported by its maintenance, repair, and overhaul (MRO) operations (a.k.a. Aerospace Products). Leveraging its refurbishment and manufacturing capacity, FTAI can purchase an engine, refurbish it to sell or strip several of them for their parts, and reconfigure a new one from components that have not timed out. The engines that are the primary focus of FTAI are older with many in the unserviceable category. They have three modules (low-pressure turbine, core, and fan) comprised of 18 ‘life-limited’ parts that must be replaced/refurbished after an FAA-mandated set of cycles (takeoffs and landings). FTAI is attempting to leverage its MRO operation where it can mix-and-match life-limited parts with similar remaining life spans to create an engine for sale (or lease) to airlines at a significant profit. All of this functionality was located at an ex Pan-Am engine shop in South Florida until the company purchased an underutilized 526,000-square-foot aircraft engine maintenance facility in Montreal, Canada from Lockheed Martin (LMT) for $170 million in September 2024. A 50% ownership interest in an ex-Alitalia MRO facility located in Rome, Italy is expected to close in Q2 '25, eventually bringing the company’s total maintenance/refurbishment capacity to 600 engines (1,800 modules) annually.

Returning to Aviation Leasing, its typical clients are small and medium airlines, defined as less than 100 engines and 100 to 500 engines, respectively. It also houses a maintenance operation that generates significant revenue. The segment was responsible for FY24 net income from continuing operations of $210.2 million and Adj. EBITDA of $500 million on revenue of $628.4 million.

Aerospace Products consists of development and manufacture, repair/refurbishment, and sale of aircraft engines and aftermarket components for CFM56-7B, CFM56-5B, and V2500 commercial engines, which encompass ~60% of the commercial narrowbody jets in service globally. This segment currently has a 5% share of the $22 billion MRO business associated with those three engine types. However, management is looking to eventually expand this business significantly (as evidenced by its two recent MRO acquisitions) to 25%. If the facilities in Miami, Montreal, and Rome are at full capacity – a goal that will take several years – FTAI could command a 20% market share. Aerospace Products contributed FY24 net income from continuing operations of $346.3 million and Adj. EBITDA of $381 million on revenue of $1.08 billion.

Putting it all together with corporate overhead, FTAI earned $2.13 a share (non-GAAP) and Adj. EBITDA of $862.1 million on revenue of $1.73 billion in FY24. The bottom line is challenging to tease out, given that 2024 was the year the company became a pure play aviation concern, but for comparison purposes, FY23 Adj. EBITDA from continuing operations was $597.3 million on revenue of $1.17 billion, as it made significant investments into its aviation business in 2024.

Share Price Performance

FTAI was one of the best performing stocks coming out of the pandemic-induced selloff, surging 5,666% from a low of $3.15 a share to an all-time high of $181.64 on January 10, 2025. Then on January 15, 2025, Muddy Waters issued a short report on the company, accusing it of misleading financial reporting, stating that it was representing one-time engine sales (i.e., straight trading without any overhaul or module swapping) as MRO revenue (i.e., three individual module sales) in its Aerospace Products segment in an attempt to garner higher multiples than that of a traditional leasing business.

Distilling down on the short report, an MRO generally trades at a multiple to Adj. EBITDA, which is generally a higher valuation than that of a leasing business, which is typically evaluated through a price-to-book value lens. FTAI’s mix-and-match module ‘solution’ is essentially designed to create more attractive engines for the end user; specifically, ones in which all the life-limited parts time out simultaneously. In addition to suggesting that 80% of its ‘module’ swaps were whole engine sales – backed by testimony of an alleged former employee – the report suggested that the aforementioned purchase of the Lockheed MRO shop was cosmetic, as that specific operation has always struggled for business. That said, these accusations seem a bit fantastical. For example, if one life-limited part needs to be replaced in one of three modules to bring an engine back to serviceability, and it is then resold – counting as a sale of three modules – that still means the engine in question had to enter the MRO shop for the upgrade. Because the other two modules didn’t require work, the accuracy of the company’s claim that it serviced and sold three modules is the adjudication of the would-be investor.

FTAI was also accused of manipulating the depreciation schedule to generate higher EBITDA margins for its more highly valued – on any applicable multiple basis – Aerospace Products segment at the expense of its lower-valued Aviation Leasing segment. Without getting too into the weeds, this alleged manipulation has the effect of lowering the Aerospace Products segment’s purchase price for what the company deems unserviceable engines – a significant source of its engine modules. Either way, with the market suddenly buying into the narrative of FTAI being a leasing business disguised as an MRO, shares of FTAI plunged 24% in the subsequent trading session to $116.08, eventually bottoming at $75.06 on January 21st, off 59% from its all-time high in six trading sessions.

Further conflating matters for the market was the company’s Strategic Capital Initiative [SCI]. Announced at YE24, through partnerships, the enterprise allows FTAI to participate in the acquisition of on-lease narrowbody aircraft at scale. The first partnership, to which the company is contributing ~$100 million of capital, is purchasing 46 on-lease aircraft from FTAI for $549 million, with FTAI exclusively providing replacement aircraft engines and modules for the term of the collaboration. This arrangement is designed to transform FTAI into an asset-light business, highlighting the MRO segment of its operations.

The stock has been on a wild ride ever since. Management denied the Muddy Waters’ allegations and FTAI’s stock worked its way back to $158.83 a share by February 20th, the date it announced the completion of a board-directed independent audit with no 10-K filing delay. That said, a lackluster Q4 '24 financial reports featuring a revenue miss of $18.2 million on February 26th caused shares of FTAI to reverse course, trading to as low as $81.45 on April 7th.

Q1 '25 Financials & Outlook

They had bounced back to a closing price of $107.11 on April 30, 2025, when the company released its Q1 '25 financials, posting earnings of $1.06 a share (non-GAAP) and Adj. EBITDA of $268.6 million on revenue of $502.1 million, versus $0.43 a share (non-GAAP) and Adj. EBITDA of $164.1 million on revenue of $326.7 million. The top line missed Street consensus by $65.6 million, although that was likely due to the timing of asset sales to the SCI partnership.

Management’s FY25 outlook remained unchanged at free cash flow generation of $650 million on Adj. EBITDA of $1.10 billion to $1.15 billion from its two operating segments (i.e., excluding corporate overhead). Although the conference call was rather upbeat, the market tanked shares of FTAI 19% lower to $86.90 in the subsequent trading session.

Balance Sheet & Analyst Commentary

Another minor concern is the company’s net leverage, which with debt of $3.64 billion and cash of $112 million, stood at 3.7 on March 31, 2025. However, it is projected to fall to the lower end of a 3.0 to 3.5 range by YE25. FTAI pays a quarterly dividend of $0.30 a share for a current yield of 1.3%, but growth capital and debt retirement are its chief priorities for its FY25 operating cash flow.

Despite the significant turbulence in 2025, sell-side analysts are currently unanimously bullish on the company’s prospects. Since May, seven analyst firms, including BTIG, Morgan Stanley, and RBC Capital have reissued Buy ratings on the stock. Price targets proffered range from $123 to $300 a share, with the majority in the $150 to $190 range.

On average, they expect FTAI to earn $4.69 a share (non-GAAP) on revenue of $2.31 billion in FY25, followed by $6.77 a share (non-GAAP) on revenue of $2.83 billion in FY26.

Both CEO Joe Adams and CFO David Moreno appear clear on the company’s path forward, having purchased 9,580 shares between them with a $94 handle on May 2, 2025.

Verdict

Muddy Waters objections notwithstanding, the shifting of the company’s aircraft assets off of its balance sheet through joint ventures with MRO exclusivity with an endgame of lowering its debt and concentrating on commanding a larger share of the higher-margin MRO business is a sound one.

FTAI now trades at just under 27 times forward earnings. This is a slight premium to that of the overall S&P 500. It should be noted, FTAI has much faster sales and earnings growth projected than the overall index. That said, the stock is up just over 40% since the start of May

In this topsy-turvy market, I am not willing to chase that rally. If the next market decline takes FTAI back down to the low to mid $100s where the stock's valuation would be roughly in line with the S&P 500 index, I will initiate a small position in FTAI via covered call orders at that point in time.

data.title: SCHO: The Case For Rotating Out Of Short-Duration Treasuries
data.description:
The Schwab Short-Term U.S. Treasury ETF (NYSEARCA:SCHO) is a fixed income strategy designed to provide investors with exposure to the short-end of the Treasury bond yield curve.

The performance of this portfolio strategy will be sensitive to the yield curve, which has gradually become less appealing at the 2-year & 3-year duration in the last year.

Taking into consideration the yield curve, SCHO may be positioned to underperform the ultrashort-end of the curve as well as long-duration Treasuries in terms of income generation. In general, the price of a bond is valued inverse to its respective yield, making the 2-3-year Treasuries more expensive to own while offering investors less earning potential. As a result of this phenomenon, an investor may realize higher returns by investing with a barbell strategy, or investing in ultrashort- and long-duration Treasuries, rather than in short-term Treasuries.

Nonetheless, SCHO has provided investors with significantly more stability in terms of performance when compared to its intermediate (SCHR) and long-duration (SCHQ) bond funds. This is likely the result of the transmutation of the yield curve, which has significantly driven up the yield for long-duration bonds relative to the 2-3 year Treasuries. Essentially, this means that the short-term Treasuries held appreciated in value inverse to the rate decline.

Because investments are meant to be valued in a forward-looking manner, rotating into a long-duration strategy may be more optimal when considering the yield curve.

Overall, the Federal Reserve isn’t expected to cut the Federal Funds Rate in the June 2025 meeting and rather, potentially make the first cut in the September and/or December quarters. 80% of economists are expecting the Fed to cut the key rate to 3.75-4%, down from the current range of 4.25-4.50%.

This could result in a short-term return on the ultrashort-to-short end of the curve, making long-dated Treasuries a more appealing investment in terms of yield. A rate cut could impact SCHO by increasing the value of short-term Treasuries while lowering the income for new issuances. If a rate cut does occur, I believe it would be prudent to rotate into long-duration Treasuries vs. short-duration, providing investors with greater income exposure.

SCHO was designed to provide investors with exposure to the Treasury bond market with a duration of 1-3 years. Looking at the individual assets, roughly 57% of the portfolio has a duration of 1-2 years with the remainder in 2-3 years.

Accordingly, SCHO has a weighted average maturity of 2 years.

SCHO has a low-cost structure, charging investors a mere 3bps. This is in part with peer short-duration bond funds with similar strategies, including SPDR Short Term Treasury ETF (SPTS), Vanguard Short-Term Corporate Bond Index (VCSH), and Invesco Short Term Treasury ETF (TBLL).

SCHO has a weighted average coupon of 2.97% and has an average yield to maturity of 3.93%. The portfolio has a turnover rate of 69.38%, potentially adding certain duration risk to the strategy. This can be seen in the distribution rate history over the last two years, declining to $0.0874/share in the most recent monthly distribution at the beginning of June 2025. This annualizes to $1.0488/share, yielding approximately 4.32% on a forward basis.

This compares to Schwab’s long-duration Treasury strategy, SCHQ, which has been exhibiting increases to its monthly distributions, annualized at $1.7316/share, yielding approximately 5.52% on a forward basis.

Of the two options, it might be worth considering the beginning of a rotation into long-duration treasuries. Given that a rate cut may occur later in the year or next year, there may remain some value to extract from SCHO; however, for income purposes, SCHQ would potentially hold the higher ground.

Risks Related to SCHO

SCHO only invests in short-duration US Treasuries, exposing investors to the short-end of the yield curve. Though SCHO has exhibited stability in past performance, short-duration US Treasuries are exposed to changes in the Federal Funds Rate, potentially resulting in low-to-no growth during periods of rate stability. In the instance of the Fed cutting the key rate, SCHO may exhibit increased value as the existing bond book will have a more appealing yield when compared to new issuances. In turn, if rates were to rise in the case of higher inflationary pressures following the implementation of the US tariff policies, the value of short-duration Treasuries may decline.

Investors must also take into consideration duration risk. Duration risk is associated with bonds’ sensitivity to changes in interest rates. If the key rate is set to be reduced, the value of short-term bonds may appreciate. In turn, portfolio turnover must be considered as new issuances may provide investors with a lower yield, or a lower income component, making these assets less appealing when compared to higher-yielding investments.

Given the current market conditions, I recommend SCHO with a hold rating. I believe that it may be a good time for investors to rotate into longer duration Treasuries given the more appealing yield.

data.title: ADMA Biologics: Slowing Growth Warrants A Shift To Neutral (Rating Downgrade)
data.description:
Reassessment Of ADMA Biologics' Recent Results

This article serves as an update to my previous coverage of ADMA Biologics (NASDAQ:ADMA). My initial "Strong Buy" rating was based on the company's strong position in the biologics market, with the unique profile of its flagship drug, Asceniv, and a clear path to significant revenue growth and profitability. Since writing my article, ADMA stock price rose 23% while the S&P 500 remained flat.

ADMA has so far delivered impressive year-over-year growth. However, a trend has emerged in recent quarters that cannot be ignored. One can notice a flattening of sequential revenue. In my opinion, for a growth stock, momentum is key, and this recent stagnation raises questions about the company's near-term trajectory. While the long-term potential remains, I believe a more cautious, neutral stance is now better suited until the company can demonstrate a return to strong quarter-over-quarter growth.
